name: Agua Fria River Agate Beds
short_description: BLM public collecting area along the Agua Fria River near Prescott producing colorful agates and chalcedony. Free access with a 25 lb daily limit.
description:
The Agua Fria River Agate Beds are located on Bureau of Land Management (BLM) property in Yavapai County, offering rockhounders accessible public collecting opportunities without permit requirements. The site sits at 2,472 feet elevation in the transition zone between the high desert and chaparral ecosystems of central Arizona.

The geology of this area reflects the complex mineralization typical of the Agua Fria River drainage. Agate and chalcedony specimens weathered from rhyolitic and basaltic host rocks are exposed in wash gravels and along eroded hillsides. The river's seasonal flow and flash flood cycles continually turn over gravels and expose new material at the surface, making this a productive location for patient collectors.

Rockhounders can expect to find nodular agates in whites, grays, and occasional earthy tones, along with translucent chalcedony and jasper pieces. Most collectible material occurs in the active wash and surrounding terraces where gravity concentration has gathered denser stones. The rolling terrain with scattered juniper and desert scrub vegetation is typical of Yavapai County's mineral-rich zones.

This location works well for family outings and beginners because collecting is straightforward surface picking with no special equipment required. The proximity to Black Canyon City and Highway 17 makes access convenient for day trips from the Phoenix metro area or Prescott.

Be prepared for temperature extremes, especially in summer months when midday heat can exceed 95 degrees Fahrenheit. Winter and early spring offer the most comfortable collecting conditions.
tips: Focus your search in the active Agua Fria River wash and in the erosional gullies cut into adjacent hillsides where gravity has concentrated heavier agate and chalcedony material. Agates here often occur as nodules partially exposed in wash gravels or weathered loose on the surface. A small hand rake or brush is helpful for clearing gravels and increasing visibility, and a 5-gallon bucket makes transport easier. Look for the subtle color banding and translucency that distinguishes true agate from ordinary quartz; specimens often show whites, grays, and earth tones rather than vivid colors.
terrain_notes: Elevation 2,472 feet with gently rolling terrain interspersed with small ridges. Vegetation consists of scattered juniper, palo verde, and desert shrubs. The active wash floor is relatively easy walking with mixed sandy-gravelly substrate and occasional smooth rock outcrops. Bank slopes range from gentle to moderately steep in some areas. Footing is generally good on established wash, but side slopes can be rocky and loose in places. Parking areas to active collecting zone typically involve walks of 0.25 to 1 mile.
best_season: October - April. Winter and early spring offer comfortable daytime temperatures (60 - 75 degrees Fahrenheit) and lower risk of flash flooding. Summer temperatures regularly exceed 95 degrees and pose heat hazard risk. Late summer monsoons (July - September) bring flash flood danger to washes.
written_directions: From Black Canyon City, take Interstate 17 north for approximately 5 miles toward Cordes Junction. Look for unsigned or informally marked pullouts along the highway shoulders near mile marker areas where washboard access roads descend toward the Agua Fria River drainage. Alternatively, from Prescott, take Arizona Highway 169 (Williamson Valley Road) south and east, following West New River Road (approximately 9 miles from the location) toward the river breaks. Access points are typically unmarked; local rockhounding knowledge or consultation with the Prescott Area BLM field office is recommended for current best entry points. Four-wheel-drive or high-clearance vehicle recommended for rough wash approaches.
road_conditions: Highway 17 is paved. Access roads to the site are typically rough, washboarded dirt roads with potential rocky sections and wash crossings. High-clearance vehicle strongly recommended; standard passenger vehicles may risk undercarriage damage.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along highway shoulders or scattered turnouts near wash access points. No developed parking lot. Capacity varies, typically accommodates 3 - 8 vehicles per access point. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collection is limited to casual surface picking and hand tools. BLM regulations apply; respect all boundary markers and avoid any active mining claims.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
Respect claim boundaries
nearest_services: Black Canyon City, 6 miles away (population 2,677), has gas stations, food, and water. Prescott, 30 miles, offers full services.

faq.question: What types of agates can you find at Agua Fria River Agate Beds near Prescott?
faq.answer: Collectors at Agua Fria River Agate Beds primarily find nodular agates in whites, grays, and earth tones, along with translucent chalcedony and jasper. Specimens vary in size from pea-sized to hand-sized, with the best material weathered loose in the active wash gravels. The Agua Fria River drainage's geology produces classic desert agates with banding and translucent windows typical of Arizona's mineral-rich central highlands.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Agua Fria River free, and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Agua Fria River Agate Beds is completely free. The site is on BLM public land, and no permit is required for recreational collecting. You are limited to 25 lbs per day of personal use collection under FLPMA (Federal Land Policy and Management Act) regulations. Commercial collection and motorized excavation are not allowed.

faq.question: How far is Agua Fria River Agate Beds from Prescott, and what's the drive like?
faq.answer: Agua Fria River Agate Beds is approximately 30 miles south of Prescott via Interstate 17 toward Black Canyon City, roughly a 45-minute drive. From Black Canyon City (6 miles from the site), you access the location via rough, washboarded dirt roads and wash crossings. A high-clearance or four-wheel-drive vehicle is strongly recommended, as standard passenger vehicles risk undercarriage damage.

faq.question: What's the best time of year to rockhound at Agua Fria River Agate Beds?
faq.answer: October through April is the ideal collecting season. Daytime temperatures range from 60 to 75 degrees Fahrenheit, making long days in the field comfortable. Summer temperatures exceed 95 degrees and pose heat hazards. Late summer monsoons (July - September) bring dangerous flash flood risk to the wash, which can destroy equipment and create life-threatening conditions.

faq.question: Is Agua Fria River Agate Beds suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Agua Fria River Agate Beds is beginner and family-friendly. Collecting requires only casual surface picking with hand tools - no special skills, experience, or heavy equipment needed. The terrain is moderate and mostly walkable. The site is accessible within a day trip from Phoenix (60 miles) or Prescott (30 miles), making it convenient for weekend outings. Always bring plenty of water and sun protection.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should you bring rockhounding at Agua Fria River?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand rake or brush to clear gravels and improve visibility of agates in the wash. A 5-gallon bucket helps transport the 25 lbs of daily collection. Wear sturdy boots with good ankle support for rocky, uneven terrain. Essential supplies include at least 2 - 3 liters of water per person, sunscreen, a hat, and a headlamp for early morning or late afternoon collecting. A GPS device or smartphone map app helps navigate rough approach roads safely.

meta_title: Agua Fria River Agate Beds - Arizona Rockhounding
meta_description: Find agates and chalcedony at Agua Fria River Agate Beds near Prescott. Free BLM public land, no permit needed. Open year-round for collectors.
